1 Introduction

Here we will be wrangling messy data files on water quality and predatory fish species from the Ross River estuary fish surveys. We know that there are 4 survey sites, 4 species, and 30 days of sampling. One of the files contains metadata on the sites and sensor data on the environmental conditions at each site.

2 Housekeeping

Purge the global environment with the command rm(list = ls()).

3 Load packages

The tidyverse and readxl package libraries must be installed and loaded for this exercise.

4 Loading and Cleaning the data

4.1 Load catch data

The catch data is divided across 4 sheets in one excel file.

Code 
# Load sheets individually:
catch1 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 1)
catch2 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 2)
catch3 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 3)
catch4 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 4)

4.2 Inspect individual sheets

Code 
print(catch1)
# A tibble: 104 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        6
 2 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     3

# ℹ 100 more rows

There are variations in the species and site names, but they otherwise look to be the same. We can clean after binding all the rows together.

4.3 Bind rows together

Code 
catch_data <- bind_rows(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)
# Clean up the environment
rm(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)

4.4 Examine catch data

# A tibble: 427 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        6
 2 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     3
 3 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream             4
 4 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          2
 5 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barramundi        1
 6 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     1
 7 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 bream             3
 8 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead          3
 9 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     2
10 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathead          3
# ℹ 417 more rows

We want to know how many levels of site and species we have, which we can’t tell from this output. One option is to check these columns explicitly using distinct().

Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site      
  <chr>     
1 Lower_ross
2 Mid_Ross  
3 ROSS MOUTH
4 Upper Ross
Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  species      
  <chr>        
1 barramundi   
2 MANGROVE JACK
3 bream        
4 flathead

4.4.1 Next steps for catch data

We need the site names to have a uniform format. It looks like sites correspond to unique zones on the Ross River, but we need to load the metadata to confirm this. We can use the stringr package to make site names consistent so we can then load the taxonomic dictionary file to translate common to scientific species names.

4.5 Load metadata

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  site_name    lat   lon zone      
  <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <chr>     
1 Upper_Ross -19.3  147. Upstream  
2 mid_ross   -19.3  147. Middle    
3 LOWER ROSS -19.3  147. Downstream
4 ross_mouth -19.2  147. Marine

4.6 Clean metadata

Code 
metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site_name 
  <chr>     
1 Upper_Ross
2 mid_ross  
3 LOWER ROSS
4 ross_mouth

Site names in metadata are all over the place. We need to standardize them so we can join this table with the catch data table.

4.6.1 Standardize site names in catch data and metadata

Aside from simply standardizing the site names, we also want to convert the character variables to factors.

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site = str_replace_all(site, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site = str_to_lower(site)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))
Code 
metadata <- metadata |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site_name = str_to_lower(site_name)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))

4.6.2 Recheck site names

Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site      
  <fct>     
1 lower_ross
2 mid_ross  
3 ross_mouth
4 upper_ross
Code 
metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site_name 
  <fct>     
1 upper_ross
2 mid_ross  
3 lower_ross
4 ross_mouth

Site names are now standardized across the catch data and metadata files.

4.7 Load taxonomic dictionary

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  common_name   scientific_name          
  <chr>         <chr>                    
1 barramundi    Lates calcarifer         
2 mangrove_jack Lutjanus argentimaculatus
3 bream         Acanthopagrus spp.       
4 flathead      Platycephalus spp.

4.8 Standardize species (common) names in catch data

The common species names are already in a standardized format. We need to standardize the species names in the catch data to match.

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(
    species = str_to_lower(str_trim(species)),
    species = str_replace_all(species, pattern = " ", replacement = "_")
  )

catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  species      
  <chr>        
1 barramundi   
2 mangrove_jack
3 bream        
4 flathead

4.9 Create new variable (“scientific_name”) in catch log by joining with taxonomic dictionary

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(common_name = species) |>
  left_join(dictionary, by = join_by(common_name))
# A tibble: 427 × 6
   site       date                species      count common_name scientific_name
   <fct>      <dttm>              <chr>        <dbl> <chr>       <chr>          
 1 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi       6 barramundi  Lates calcarif…
 2 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 mangrove_ja…     3 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen…
 3 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream            4 bream       Acanthopagrus …
 4 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead         2 flathead    Platycephalus …
 5 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barramundi       1 barramundi  Lates calcarif…
 6 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 mangrove_ja…     1 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen…
 7 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 bream            3 bream       Acanthopagrus …
 8 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead         3 flathead    Platycephalus …
 9 lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 mangrove_ja…     2 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen…
10 lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathead         3 flathead    Platycephalus …
# ℹ 417 more rows

Looks good - the species and site names are fixed.

4.10 Load sensor data

Rows: 11,520
Columns: 5
$ site        <chr> "upper_ross", "mid_ross", "lower_ross", "ross_mouth", "upp…
$ timestamp   <chr> "01/05/2026 00:00", "01/05/2026 00:00", "01/05/2026 00:00"…
$ temperature <dbl> 23.71379, 24.13302, 24.23558, 24.64147, 26.06955, 24.62576…
$ salinity    <dbl> 4.054812, 15.742962, 28.934938, 33.891350, 3.962750, 12.05…
$ turbidity   <dbl> 6.489046, 49.216967, 48.135752, 49.811493, 25.184739, 34.8…
  • Sonde data was collected every 15 minutes for the same 4 sites across the 30 days of sampling. The data was sourced in a daily format, and outliers were removed.
    • Note that timestamp is a character-type column. We will probably want to parse this to a date or date-time column.
  • Three different environmental variables were measured: temperature, salinity, and turbidity.
    • Environmental variables need cleaning: some negative salinity values, some -999 turbidity values (failed values).
  • Could investigate effects of all three on fish spp abundance or pick one? Need to think about prior research and hypotheses etc.

4.11 Wrangle sensor data

Using the mutate() verb, we can create a POSIXct-type column for the timestamp data, change all -999 turbidity values to true NA values, and change all negative salinity values to be NA.

Code 
sensor_data <- sensor_raw |> 
    mutate(
      # Parse the character string to Day/Month/Year Hour:Minute
      datetime = dmy_hm(timestamp),
      # Chance -999.0 to NA values
      turbidity = na_if(turbidity, -999),
      # Convert negative salinity values to NA
      salinity = ifelse(salinity < 0, NA, salinity)
    )

print(sensor_data)
# A tibble: 11,520 × 6
   site       timestamp       temperature salinity turbidity datetime           
   <chr>      <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dttm>             
 1 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        23.7     4.05      6.49 2026-05-01 00:00:00
 2 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.1    15.7      49.2  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 3 lower_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        24.2    28.9      48.1  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 4 ross_mouth 01/05/2026 00:…        24.6    33.9      49.8  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 5 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        26.1     3.96     25.2  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 6 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.6    12.1      34.8  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 7 lower_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        22.9    30.9      33.9  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 8 ross_mouth 01/05/2026 00:…        20.6    33.9      27.4  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 9 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        24.9     4.32     13.0  2026-05-01 00:30:00
10 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.3    17.4      31.9  2026-05-01 00:30:00
# ℹ 11,510 more rows

4.11.1 Summarize 15-minute sensor data into daily means

We want to create a second data table of the sensor data summarized by date.

  1. Mutate the datetime variable to create a date column .
  2. Group by site and date.
  3. Use the summarise() verb to create a column for mean temperature, mean salinity, and mean turbidity for each site on each date.
Code 
sensor_daily <- sensor_data |>
  mutate(date = as_date(floor_date(datetime, "day"))) |>
  group_by(site, date) |>
  summarise(
    mean_temp = mean(temperature, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_salinity = mean(salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_turbidity = mean(turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")

print(sensor_daily)
# A tibble: 120 × 5
   site       date       mean_temp mean_salinity mean_turbidity
   <chr>      <date>         <dbl>         <dbl>          <dbl>
 1 lower_ross 2026-05-01      23.8          28.4           27.9
 2 lower_ross 2026-05-02      24.0          27.7           24.1
 3 lower_ross 2026-05-03      24.2          28.1           20.6
 4 lower_ross 2026-05-04      24.0          28.2           28.4
 5 lower_ross 2026-05-05      23.9          27.6           26.7
 6 lower_ross 2026-05-06      24.0          28.2           24.0
 7 lower_ross 2026-05-07      24.0          27.6           25.1
 8 lower_ross 2026-05-08      24.0          28.0           25.9
 9 lower_ross 2026-05-09      24.0          27.8           24.6
10 lower_ross 2026-05-10      23.9          28.1           26.2
# ℹ 110 more rows

5 Join catch data with metadata and sensor data

Left join the catch data with the metadata and daily sensor data tables to create a finalized data table with all relevant information.

Code 
catch_data_final <- catch_data |> 
  left_join(metadata, by = join_by(site == site_name)) |> 
  left_join(sensor_daily, by = join_by(site, date))

glimpse(catch_data_final)
Rows: 427
Columns: 12
$ site            <chr> "lower_ross", "lower_ross", "lower_ross", "lower_ross"…
$ date            <dttm> 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-…
$ species         <chr> "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "bream", "flathead", "b…
$ count           <dbl> 6, 3, 4, 2, 1, 1, 3, 3, 2, 3, 1, 8, 4, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2, …
$ common_name     <chr> "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "bream", "flathead", "b…
$ scientific_name <chr> "Lates calcarifer", "Lutjanus argentimaculatus", "Acan…
$ lat             <dbl> -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26…
$ lon             <dbl> 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81…
$ zone            <fct> Downstream, Downstream, Downstream, Downstream, Downst…
$ mean_temp       <dbl> 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.98099, 23.9…
$ mean_salinity   <dbl> 28.35281, 28.35281, 28.35281, 28.35281, 27.74122, 27.7…
$ mean_turbidity  <dbl> 27.90740, 27.90740, 27.90740, 27.90740, 24.09800, 24.0…

Note that we only have 427 rows of data…4 species, 4 sites, and 30 surveys = 4 * 4 * 30 = 480! There should be 480 rows in our data table.

Some days there were no species counted at some sites, so these rows were omitted from the data. We need to wrangle the data so that zero counts are represented as 0 and not NA. This is important when it comes time to visualizing the data because we want to be able to distinguish between true absences (0) and missing data (NA).

6 Wrangle final dataset

Code 
catch_data_final <- catch_data_final |> 
  complete(
    nesting(site, date, 
           # lat, lon, 
            zone, mean_temp, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity), scientific_name
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Convert the resulting NA counts to 0
    count = coalesce(count, 0)
  )

glimpse(catch_data_final)
Rows: 480
Columns: 12
$ site            <chr> "lower_ross", "lower_ross", "lower_ross", "lower_ross"…
$ date            <dttm> 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-…
$ zone            <fct> Downstream, Downstream, Downstream, Downstream, Downst…
$ mean_temp       <dbl> 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.79137, 23.98099, 23.9…
$ mean_salinity   <dbl> 28.35281, 28.35281, 28.35281, 28.35281, 27.74122, 27.7…
$ mean_turbidity  <dbl> 27.90740, 27.90740, 27.90740, 27.90740, 24.09800, 24.0…
$ scientific_name <chr> "Acanthopagrus spp.", "Lates calcarifer", "Lutjanus ar…
$ species         <chr> "bream", "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "flathead", "b…
$ count           <dbl> 4, 6, 3, 2, 3, 1, 1, 3, 0, 0, 2, 3, 4, 1, 8, 1, 1, 1, …
$ common_name     <chr> "bream", "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "flathead", "b…
$ lat             <dbl> -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26, -19.26…
$ lon             <dbl> 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81, 146.81…

WE HAVE SUCCESSFULLY WRANGLED THE DATA!

7 Analyzing the Data

We first want to check if there is an actual difference in the environmental variables among zones.

7.1 Salinity

Code 
sal_sum <- catch_data_final |> 
  group_by(zone) |> 
  summarise(
    # se_salinity = sd(mean_salinity) / sqrt(n()),
    sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(sal_sum)
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  zone       sd_salinity median_salinity
  <fct>            <dbl>           <dbl>
1 Upstream         0.188            4.99
2 Middle           0.394           15.0 
3 Downstream       0.349           28.0 
4 Marine           0.103           34.0
Code 
# Or with plotrix
# library(plotrix)
# catch_data_final |> 
#   group_by(scientific_name, zone) |> 
#   summarise(
#     se_salinity = std.error(count),
#     # sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     .groups = "drop"
#   )
Code 
sal_sum |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_salinity)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_salinity - sd_salinity, ymax = median_salinity + sd_salinity), width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Salinity by Zone with Standard Deviation Error Bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Salinity (ppt)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

As expected, there are significant differences in salinity between zones of the Ross River. We also know there are differences between species in terms of their response to salinity gradients.

7.2 Turbidity

Code 
turb_sum <- catch_data_final |>
  group_by(zone) |>
  summarise(
    sd_turbidity = sd(mean_turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_turbidity = median(mean_turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(turb_sum)
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  zone       sd_turbidity median_turbidity
  <fct>             <dbl>            <dbl>
1 Upstream           1.63             25.2
2 Middle             1.25             25.7
3 Downstream         1.64             25.2
4 Marine             1.27             25.5
Code 
turb_sum |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_turbidity)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_turbidity - sd_turbidity, ymax = median_turbidity + sd_turbidity), width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Turbidity by Zone with Standard Deviation Error Bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Turbidity (NTU)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

7.3 Temperature

Code 
temp_sum <- catch_data_final |>
  group_by(zone) |>
  summarise(
    sd_temperature = sd(mean_temp, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_temperature = median(mean_temp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(temp_sum)
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  zone       sd_temperature median_temperature
  <fct>               <dbl>              <dbl>
1 Upstream            0.140               24.0
2 Middle              0.129               24.0
3 Downstream          0.126               24.0
4 Marine              0.171               24.0
Code 
temp_sum |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_temperature)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_temperature - sd_temperature, ymax = median_temperature + sd_temperature), 
                width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Temperature by Zone with Standard Deviation Error Bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Temperature (°C)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

We’ll choose salinity as our main environmental variable of interest, but we know that there are also differences in turbidity and temperature across the zones, which could also be influencing fish abundance patterns. We can discuss this in the interpretation of our results and consider potential confounding effects of these other environmental variables in our discussion.

7.4 Remake salinity plot nicely for publication

Code 
sal_sum |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_salinity)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_salinity - sd_salinity, ymax = median_salinity + sd_salinity), width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Salinity by Zone with Standard Deviation Error Bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Salinity (ppt)")

7.5 Patterns in Fish Abundance

7.5.1 Calculate summary stats

Code 
fish_stats <- catch_data_final |>
  group_by(zone, scientific_name) |>
  summarise(
    mean_catch = mean(count),
    sem_catch = sd(count) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(fish_stats)
# A tibble: 16 × 4
   zone       scientific_name           mean_catch sem_catch
   <fct>      <chr>                          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1 Upstream   Acanthopagrus spp.              2.57     0.302
 2 Upstream   Lates calcarifer                4.8      0.309
 3 Upstream   Lutjanus argentimaculatus       1.9      0.211
 4 Upstream   Platycephalus spp.              2.3      0.333
 5 Middle     Acanthopagrus spp.              2.23     0.286
 6 Middle     Lates calcarifer                2.2      0.242
 7 Middle     Lutjanus argentimaculatus       1.97     0.206
 8 Middle     Platycephalus spp.              1.97     0.265
 9 Downstream Acanthopagrus spp.              2        0.275
10 Downstream Lates calcarifer                1.6      0.256
11 Downstream Lutjanus argentimaculatus       2        0.299
12 Downstream Platycephalus spp.              2.1      0.175
13 Marine     Acanthopagrus spp.              2.33     0.344
14 Marine     Lates calcarifer                1.57     0.248
15 Marine     Lutjanus argentimaculatus       1.5      0.208
16 Marine     Platycephalus spp.              5.67     0.237

7.5.2 Summary stats plot

Code 
fish_stats |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = mean_catch, fill = zone)) +
  geom_line(group = 1) +
  # add in points from master data
  geom_point(data = catch_data_final, aes(x = zone, y = count), 
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.2), alpha = 0.2, size = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  
  geom_point(position = "dodge", size = 4, pch = 23) +
  
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_catch - sem_catch, ymax = mean_catch + sem_catch), 
                position = position_dodge(0.9), width = 0.2) +
  
  facet_wrap(~ scientific_name, scales = "fixed") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "italic", size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Predatory Fish Abundance across Estuarine Salinity Gradients",
    caption = "Ross River Estuary System, May 2026",
    x = "Spatial Zone",
    y = "Abundance (per day)",
    fill = "Spatial Zone"
    )

From this plot we can see that Acanthopagrus spp. and Lutjanus argentimaculatus have very a very similar abundance across the different spatial zones of the Ross River. Lates calcarifer was twice as abundant in the upstream spatial zone compared to all other zones, implying that this species prefers very low salinity concentrations. Platycephalus spp. was twice as abundant in the marine spatial zone than all other zones, implying that this species prefers high salinity concentrations.
